topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the graduate seminar room seemed to hum with a particular, unwelcome intensity. I was midway through explaining my methodology, a carefully constructed edifice of theoretical reasoning, when Dr. Albright cleared his throat. It was a sound that usually signaled approval, but today, it felt like the starting gun for a very public execution. He leaned back in his chair, the leather groaning under the sudden shift in weight, and a slow, almost deliberate smile spread across his face. "Well," he began, his voice resonating with a strange mixture of finality and amusement, "it seems my tenure here is drawing to a close." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, instantly constricting my chest. I gripped the edge of the mahogany table so tightly my knuckles bleached white. My carefully rehearsed opening statement dissolved into a slurry of panicked murmurs. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush creeping up my cheeks that I desperately tried to mask by staring intently at the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunbeam slicing through the window.

[story 2]

I found myself hovering near the faculty lounge, pretending to be deeply engrossed in a textbook, when the news finally filtered through the hushed whispers of the department. Dr. Albright was departing. The realization hit me with the force of a physical blow, knocking the air from my lungs. My hands, which had been resting casually on my backpack straps, began to tremble slightly. I felt a profound awkwardness wash over me, a sensation like being suddenly exposed under harsh scrutiny. When a colleague, Professor Davies, caught my eye, I immediately looked down at my shoes, wishing the floor would swallow me whole. "Oh, I... I didn't realize," I managed, my voice coming out thin and reedy, betraying none of the churning anxiety inside. The silence that followed felt vast, amplifying the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.

[story 3]

The announcement came during the departmental meeting, a casual declaration that somehow managed to feel monumental. Dr. Albright was stepping down. Every single person in the room seemed to freeze, and I watched, paralyzed, as the air thickened with unspoken acknowledgment of the change. My mouth went dry. I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, and I instinctively shifted my posture, trying to shrink into the nearest corner of the room. When the chairperson directed a question toward me, my throat seized up. I opened my mouth, intending to offer a measured response, but only a pathetic squeak escaped. I could feel the scrutiny of the room—the collective, silent assessment of my failure to navigate this sudden shift in the academic landscape gracefully. I wanted to disappear into the patterned wallpaper.
